title: Erasing photons from bright squeezed vacuum light via above-threshold ionization
authors: J. Rivera-Dean, T. Rook, G. Singh, P. Stammer, M. Khokhlova, E. Pisanty, C. Figueira de Morisson Faria
abstract: While the interface between strong-field physics and quantum optics offers a unique regime for combining extreme nonlinearity with quantum optical resources, its potential for generating non-classical states of light remains largely unexplored. Standard protocols for generating optical Schrödinger cat states, such as photon subtraction from squeezed light, are inherently limited in the achievable macroscopicity of the state and its scalability. In this work, we bridge this gap by demonstrating that above-threshold ionization driven by bright squeezed light provides a strong-field analogue of photon subtraction, where photoelectron detection acts as a high-intensity heralding mechanism, enabling the generation of large amplitude optical Schrödinger cat states. We characterize the resulting non-Gaussian features and show that they can be tuned via the detected photoelectron momentum, and study their robustness against the experimental imperfections arising from finite momentum resolution at the heralding step. Despite the noise, we show that the generated states can be manipulated to violate a Bell inequality, thereby highlighting their potential for foundational and practical applications. Our results establish strong-field processes as a scalable platform for macroscopic quantum state engineering, opening a route to quantum optics in previously inaccessible regimes.
summary: This paper shows that the process of Above-Threshold Ionization (ATI) driven by squeezed light can serve as a powerful, scalable mechanism analogous to photon subtraction in quantum optics. By detecting photoelectrons, the system filters the quantum state to generate large-amplitude optical Schrödinger cat states. These states exhibit clear non-classical features, allowing for the violation of Bell inequalities, thus opening strong-field physics for quantum state engineering.
main_problem: The primary challenge is generating large-amplitude optical Schrödinger cat states, as standard photon subtraction protocols are limited in scalability and achievable macroscopicity.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that Above-Threshold Ionization (ATI) driven by bright squeezed light acts as a scalable, strong-field analogue of photon subtraction, enabling the generation of large-amplitude cat states that can violate a Bell inequality.
method: The work employs a combination of quantum optics theory (photon subtraction formalism) and strong-field physics (ATI path integrals) to establish an analogy between the two processes.
model_or_system: The system involves bright squeezed vacuum light interacting with atoms undergoing Above-Threshold Ionization (ATI). The theoretical framework models the post-selected optical state $|\Phi(\mathbf{p}_f, t)angle$ resulting from photoelectron detection.
key_observables: Wigner function negativity, violation of Bell inequalities (CHSH value), and the structure of the photoelectron probability amplitude $	ilde{c}(\alpha)$.
important_parameters: The squeezing strength ($r$), the detected photoelectron momentum ($\mathbf{p}_f$), and the finite momentum resolution ($\sigma_\mu$) are key parameters analyzed.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on semiclassical approximations (saddle-point methods) and assumes that the photoelectron detection acts as a highly selective, non-linear filter on the quantum state.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the comparison between photon subtraction and photoelectron heralding, show the transformation of probability amplitudes, and display Wigner functions confirming cat-like features and Bell inequality violations.
paper_structure: The paper builds by first establishing the theoretical analogy between photon subtraction and ATI heralding. It then uses path integrals and semiclassical methods to derive the post-selected state, characterizes its non-Gaussian features, and finally tests its non-classicality by violating a Bell inequality while analyzing the impact of experimental noise.
why_interesting: This work bridges fundamental quantum optics concepts (cat states, photon subtraction) with high-intensity, non-linear strong-field physics (ATI), proposing a novel, scalable platform for generating macroscopic quantum states.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Pictorial representation highlighting the similarities between photon subtraction and photoelectron heralding. In the first case (①), a low intensity squeezed vacuum state passes through a high transmissivity beam splitter; the de- tection of a single-photon state on the reflected mode heralds the presence of a small optical Schrödinger cat state in the transmitted one. In the second case (②), a bright squeezed vacuum state interacts with an atomic system; the generation of high-energy photoelectrons heralds the presence of a large optical Schrödinger cat state. In both cases, the detection of either a single photon on the reflected mode or high-energy electrons effectively induces...

FIG. 2. Norm of the coherent state probability amplitudes before [|c(α)|, panel (a)] and after [|ct(α)|, panel (b)] pho- ton subtraction for a squeezed state input. Panel (c) shows the expectation value of different operators ˆO with respect to ˆρ = |Φ⟩⟨Φ| in Eq. (5). Specifically, we display the purity ( ˆO = ˆρ, black curve with circular markers); the probability of detecting a single photon in the reflected mode ( ˆO = |1⟩⟨1|, red curve with upward triangles); and the probability of de- tecting any other state than a vacuum state in the reflected mode ( ˆO = 1 −|0⟩⟨0|, blue curve with downward triangles).

FIG. 3. Norm of the coherent state probability amplitudes before [panel (a)] and after [panel (b), (c)] the ATI heralding. In panel (b), the heralding is performed for pf = 0, while in panel (c) for pf = (0.5∥, 0⊥). The vertical dashed lines high- light the location of the ATI rings, computed as indicated in the inset of panel (c). The numerical evaluation is carried out for r = 10 and g(ωL) = 10−6 a.u., considering a light-matter interaction lasting four optical cycles (ωL = 0.057 a.u.) and involving hydrogen atoms (Ip ≃0.5 a.u.). A discrete coher- ent state expansion is employed on a rectangular lattice with spacing k = 0.2√π (see Supplementary Material C).

FIG. 4. Solutions to Eqs. (22) and (23) for (a) pf = 0 and (b) pf = (0.5∥, 0⊥). The real (blue circles) and imaginary (red squares) parts of εx are shown as a function of the real part of the ionization time, with the opacity indicating the squeezing strength r. The electric field is depicted by the green curves: the darker curve corresponds to α > 0, while the lighter one to α < 0, highlighting the presence of two solutions associated with different regions of phase-space. In this analysis, we set g(ωL) = 10−8 a.u., ωL = 0.057 a.u., and used hydrogen (Ip ≃ 0.5 a.u.) as the atomic system.

FIG. 5. Non-Gaussian properties of the generated state, where each row presents a different measure. (a)-(c) Wigner functions of the ATI-heralded state for photoelectrons with p∥= 0, 0.1, 1.0 a.u., respectively, with p⊥= 0 a.u. held fixed in all cases. (d)-(f) Photon number probability distributions corresponding to the Wigner functions shown in the upper row, with the green (orange) curve connecting the even (odd) photon numbers. (g) Wigner negativity (green curve) of the resulting states as a function of the photoelectron energy, with the dark curve showing the photoelectron probability (nor- malized to maximum), and the gray one the probability of having even photon numbers in the state....
